name: Quetzalcoatl Serpent
description: Quetzalcoatl, the Feathered Serpent, is one of the most important deities in the Aztec pantheon. He represents the union of the earth and the sky, embodying both earthly and spiritual realms. He is a god of wisdom, knowledge, learning, and the wind. Quetzalcoatl is often depicted as a serpent covered in vibrant quetzal feathers, a symbol of his connection to the heavens. He is credited with the creation of humanity in some accounts, descending into the underworld to gather the bones of previous creations. He is seen as a bringer of civilization, teaching humans agriculture, arts, and sciences. He is also a figure of prophecy, with his return marking the beginning of a new era. His benevolent nature contrasts with the more fearsome aspects of other Aztec gods, emphasizing his role as a benefactor of humankind. The serpent form represents the earth and primal energy, while the feathers symbolize spirituality and the heavens, showcasing the duality and balance he represents. He is a patron of priests and artisans, inspiring creativity and intellectual pursuits. Quetzalcoatl's legacy is deeply woven into Aztec culture and continues to resonate today.
subtitle: Feathered Serpent God of Wisdom
appearance: Quetzalcoatl is typically depicted as a large serpent covered in the iridescent green feathers of the quetzal bird. These feathers cover his entire body, giving him a vibrant and majestic appearance. He sometimes has a human face within the serpent's mouth or wears a headdress adorned with quetzal feathers. His eyes are often described as being bright and intelligent, reflecting his wisdom. In some depictions, he has wings made of feathers, allowing him to soar through the sky. The scales of his serpent body are often intricately patterned, representing the complexity of the natural world. He is a symbol of beauty and power.
habitat: Quetzalcoatl is a celestial being and doesn't have a specific terrestrial habitat. He is associated with the sky and the heavens, often depicted flying above the earth or residing in the highest realms. He is also connected to temples and sacred places dedicated to his worship. He can manifest wherever his influence is needed, bridging the gap between the earthly and spiritual realms.
behavior: Quetzalcoatl is generally depicted as a benevolent and wise deity. He is a teacher and benefactor of humanity, bringing knowledge and civilization to the world. He is patient and compassionate, guiding humans towards a better understanding of the universe. While powerful, he is not prone to violence or aggression, preferring to use his wisdom and diplomacy to resolve conflicts. He is a patron of the arts and sciences, inspiring creativity and intellectual pursuits. He is also a figure of prophecy, guiding the Aztecs with his visions of the future.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Humanity
keyMyths.description: Quetzalcoatl descended into the underworld, Mictlan, to retrieve the bones of previous humans. He then mixed the bones with his own blood to create a new generation of humans, giving them life and intelligence.
keyMyths.source: Histoy of the Indies of New Spain

keyMyths.title: Quetzalcoatl's Exile
keyMyths.description: Tricked and deceived by Tezcatlipoca, Quetzalcoatl was forced to leave his city, Tula. He sailed eastward on a raft of serpents, promising to return one day, a prophecy linked to the arrival of the Spanish.

sources.author: Bernardino de Sahagún
sources.work: General History of the Things of New Spain (Florentine Codex)
sources.description: A comprehensive account of Aztec culture and religion, including detailed descriptions of Quetzalcoatl.
sources.text: A comprehensive account of Aztec culture and religion, including detailed descriptions of Quetzalcoatl.

sources.author: Miguel León-Portilla
sources.work: Aztec Thought and Culture: A Study of the Ancient Nahuatl Mind
sources.description: An analysis of Aztec philosophy and worldview, including the significance of Quetzalcoatl.
